speaker: interviewer
text: Wait one moment. Stop. June 1, 1995. Irving Rubinstein. Rubinstein. Diane R. Ferdinand, Chicago, Illinois. United States. English. A lav or boom mic on channel one. Camera mic on channel two. Non-drop frame.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're running.

speaker: interviewer
text: I am Diane Ferdinand. D-I-A-N-E F-E-R-D-I-N-A-N-D. I'm conducting an interview with Irving Rubinstein. Today we're in Chicago, Illinois. It's June 1, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Whenever you want to start.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please give your name, and spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Irving Rubinstein. I-R-V-I-N-G, Irving. R-U-B-I-N-S-T-E-I-N, Rubinstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth? And please spell.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Ajzik, A-J-Z-I-K, Rubinstein, R-U-B-I-N-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eisig.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same way. E-I-S-I-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 8, the 15, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your city of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kielce, Poland. K-I-E-L-C-E, Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a child and adolescent before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like before the war? Who was in your household?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a large family. I had father, mother, and four brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Leon Rubinstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Cyrla, C-Y-R-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother's name was Morris. My second brother's name was Myer. And my third brother's name was David.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's occupation was, we had a lumber mill. A very famous lumber mill in Kielce, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Demski's Lumber Mill. D-E-M-S-K-I. Demski Lumber Mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a housewife. She never worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We live in Kielce, Ulica, U-L-I-C-A, Stolarski, S-T-O-L-A-R-S-K-I, number nine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish section?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish section, yes, and it was a business section. Mostly, the whole neighborhood was known for lumber mills and woodwork.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like? I mean, if you were to take a walk through the city, what do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: A commercial neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school in Kielce, the public school for the first few years. Later, I went to a-- to a private school. And I finished public school at the seventh grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to public school by the name Sinkiwicha, S-I-N-K-I-W-I-C-H-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the private school?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the private school I went, [? Toshia ?]. I don't know the spelling. [? Toshia. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. That's fine. What-- what was your schooling? Did you switch from the public school to the private school, instead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I switched from-- from public school to private school. And there's times where you're no good, I switch back to public school, and I graduated from public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the seventh grade. Then I went to work with my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the private school a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the private-- the private school was strictly Jewish. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why did you switch from a public school to a Jewish school, and then switch back to a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my father wants me to have better education. Was a time where he could afford and was a time where he couldn't afford.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My whole family was strictly Orthodox. Religious, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your-- now, you were a child and an adolescent. What was your experience with people in the town? Could you go wherever you wanted to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In our city, before the war, we had freedom. Yeah. We had movement. We could go wherever we want. Wasn't much of a problem before the war, living in Kielce. Life was usually, I would call it normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- did you experience any anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, before the war, anti-Semitism-- anti-Semitism to some degree, but it was livable. We could survive. What happened to you, in particular? Anti-Semitic acts? Or-- how were you aware of the anti-Semitism? Time to times, used to be waves, where the Polish people used to write on the walls, [SPEAKING POLISH], 'Jews to Palestine,' or 'Jews to Madagascar.' Used to be different anti-Semitic slogans every few years. Yeah, and then quiet down. And life went on and on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they-- so they were saying, we'd like you to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any acts again Jews? Or-- did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had non-Jewish-- mostly our friends were non-Jewish. Yeah. But we used to get along pretty good. Was no problem. Was certain groups of anti-Semite, who used to spread hate time to time. Used to be waves. Came a wave. Less than two, three weeks, blow over, and it was peaceful again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: To some degree, we used to be affected. But as time went, everything straightens out [INAUDIBLE] and life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What activities or organizations were you involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly we belonged to Jewish organizations, mostly Orthodox. We also have Jewish organizations like [INAUDIBLE], Masada, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. And the zeal was future to immigrate to Israel. Mostly. All organization, we had one zeal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you join any of these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I belonged to the organization by the name Betar. Children from school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betar. B-E-T-A-R. Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in this group? Why did you join this group? You mentioned a number of different groups.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. It was a right-wing organization, with a zeal, Israel. One zeal. Only thing. To fight for Jewish state.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to have meeting. We used to have outings. We used to have gatherings. People used to speech for us. We were prepared for the future generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you join? How young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the 1934, '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about 12?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 12, 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: 13 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- why did you join?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everybody used to belong. The youth, the whole youth, belonged to organization. So everybody belonged. I joined, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my older brothers belong mostly to the same organization. I joined also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you expect to get from the organization as a 13-year-old?

speaker: interviewee
text: From an organization you mostly get--

speaker: interviewer
text: As a 13-year-old.

speaker: interviewee
text: --hope for the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was this-- I mean, what did you do that would give you hope for the future? And why did you not have hope for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the organization, they build us a future. They teach us what we should wait for the future. This was the organization's purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the meantime, what did you do while you were waiting for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: While we are waiting for the future, in 1939, the war break out where the Nazis marched into Poland. And in three days' time, they run over the whole country, and occupied Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: But between '34 and '35--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and then to '39, you've got a span of about four or five years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in this organization to prepare you for a future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had twice a weeks meetings. We had lectures. We had outings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most about the meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember too much, to be honest with you. I don't remember too much any more. It's 50 years gone. Hard To remember after 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of an organization was Betar? A fighting organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't a fighting organization. Was right-- a right-wing organization. More to the right, you know. Zionist to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it active within the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were active politically. Politically in the city. In the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do as an activist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, plain, simple member. Listen to the leaders.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember them saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's our future. We cannot build a future in Poland. Poland is an anti-Semitic country. And we have to prepare ourself to leave the country and go build a country, Israel. But they never gave us the chance to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What activities did you do to prepare you to live in Israel or in another country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people, most people used to learn trades, professions, to be prepared to make a living in the future country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What trade did you learn.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father in the lumber business, we knew how to manufacture, fabricate lumber.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you worked with your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my dad. Dad, and my brothers and the family in the lumber business, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cutting wood, measuring wood, and preparing, you know, the wood, to prefabricate difference things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did this have any relationship to what you did in Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing. There was no connection.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's nothing to do with Betar. Betar was strictly an organization. They preached the future to immigrate to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- to go back to a question I asked before, why did you choose this organization? Or were you young enough where you did what your brothers did? Do you remember making any choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was because this organization appealed to me. Has a nice appeal. The future was good. Appealed to me. That's why I joined it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What appealed?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we have to immigrate and go build a new land.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of life being tough in Kielce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Very tough. Life was very tough. Was a poor country. Was hard to make a living. And was hard to survive in a strange land. We felt like in a strange land. We'd been living in a strange land. It was hard to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel it, as a-- as a 13-year-old? What were you aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing, like my parents. Was a tough, hard life in a strange land.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there things that you wanted that you couldn't have?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we could have everything. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you treated--

speaker: interviewee
text: --we were not treated right there. We were not treated right there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what ways?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were discriminated . Persecuted time to time. Every few years, used to be pogroms on Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly we were affected. Everybody was affected. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it involve your friends? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Involved my friends, my family, and many, many other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me some examples of how you felt the mistreatment, that things weren't quite right? Something you saw or heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish people didn't like the Jewish people for one reason. The Jewish people were mostly in business and in manufacturing. And the Polish people never take a part in the economy of the country. They mostly were working people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you-- did someone say something to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. They used to say it every day. Certain, they used to complain, why the Jews control the factories, why the Jews control the business. Certainly they were jealous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make deliveries for your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever say anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they didn't say-- Usually, we used to get along fine with the Polish people. But there were anti-Semite organizations who used to spread up hate, hate against Jew, time to time. Waves. Wave of a anti-Semitic hate they used to spread time to time. But, you know, the wave passed by, and life went on and on and on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell, in advance, that a wave was about to begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. Certain-- they used to prepare it. Oh, sure. We used to feel that a wave, an anti-Semitic wave would come in time to time. Yeah, we used to feel it. And we used to struggle. We used to fight back. And it blow over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know it was coming? What would change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, used to start off the light way, you know. A little attack. We used-- used to go to a football game, you know, a soccer game. On the way from the soccer game, they used to throw stones at Jews. They used to beat up a few Jews. And we were no chicken. We used to fight back, too. We used to-- we organized in group. That's we want to be prepared to fight back. And we used to beat them up, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this have anything to do with anything you had learned-- learned in Betar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly we have to do. Betar used to teach us a lot to be defensive, to fight back. Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way? To be strong, united, and fight back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go to football games alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to go in groups. Five, 10, 20, 30, to be strong and mighty. Not to get beaten up.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end of one of these pogroms, how would things change back? What would the different be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, used to be a big fight. We fight it out, and after the fight, police came, straighten out everything, and life went back to normal in a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the police-- how did the police treat Jews? Was there a difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Police used to treat Jews fair. Yeah. The police used to follow the order of the government, yeah. They didn't stick with the hoodlums. Usually they were fair. They behaved. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you had any run-ins with the police? Did you, personally, have run-ins?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I never have any trouble with the police, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been involved in one of these fights?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure. Many times. Certainly. Many, many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it start?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they started it with us. Mostly going out from a theater, going home from a movie, or going, Saturday and Sunday, to a public park, a state park, on the way home, you know? After a concert, they used to attack us. Where the wave of anti-Semite started up. A wave, you know. Used to come and used to go. We-- we had feeling when starts, when ends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where would these things happen? In the Jewish section?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, mostly in the city section. In the park of the city. Going out from a movie. Going home from a soccer game. Going home from a parade.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about this when it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't too happy. But what can you do? You have to take the way it came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. There is no other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you expect to win in those fights?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly, we used to win. We never gave up. We were strong, united. We fought back as much as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any efforts to make things better between your-- as a 13-, 14-, 15-year-old-- to make things better in dealing with these groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly we've been trying. Sure, we've been trying, yeah. Yeah. Usually, people in the city used to get along fine-- Jews, Gentile used to get very fine. But used to the anti-Semitic groups, who used to stir up the trouble. Yeah. But the government didn't support it. Never. The government always was neutral.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was neutral.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, sure. The government never support.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there another source for anti-Semitism then, that  you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Well, a little bit, the church used to add a little, yeah. The church bear the most responsibility of the anti-Semitic outbreaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why do you say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the church used to spread rumors before every holiday. They used to spread out rumors against the Jews. And the ignorant Pollacks used to take it to their heart, and used to attack the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember any of those rumors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mostly before Easter, came out rumors that the Jews kidnap a Christian child, and killed it, and used the blood to bake matzohs for Easter, for Pesach. And this lead to pogroms. Every Easter, we used to have a pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you hear these rumors? As-- as you know, a child or an adolescent. How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Pollacks used to tell us. The Polish people were afraid. They used to tell us, why did you kidnap the Christian child to use the blood? They were not ashamed. They used to tell us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, did you see it? Can you prove it? No. I heard. Rumors used to make pogroms. When you heard that, when somebody said that to you, what did you feel was coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: We felt pretty sad. Was a bunch of ignorant people. And the people believed them that is truth. They thought this truth. And it church never tried to stop it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean to you, when-- when it happened? Did you expect, then, something to follow, when you heard a rumor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always we used to expect trouble. After the rumors, used to come trouble. Yeah, most the time, used to come trouble. Yeah. And we expected for the trouble. We cope with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any other information you'd like to share about your life before the war? Kinds of activities you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wasn't too much activities in Poland. Was a poor country. Everybody went to work. Everybody struggled to make a living. Everybody mind his own business. And we worked to make a living, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do, then. You say you were working.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working with my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're working for your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the lumber business. And we've been struggling to make a living. Was very hard to make a living. Very, very had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do specifically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Use-- use to cut lumber. Used to sort lumber. Used to deliver lumber. Used to sell. Yeah, I did the same activities, like my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you also in school at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In school, and after school. Yeah, even if I went to school, after school I went to work. Put in two, three hours' work a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then at night I went with my friends to organizations, to a park, to a movie, to a theater. Was activities in the city. Was a nice little city. Was a city of 80,000 population, and 25% of the population were Jews. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you waiting for the tape to run out?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you thinking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I always think. The head always works. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. The head works, thank God. No complaints.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think about before the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what comes to mind? Just kind of in a general-- What your life was like, you happy, sad, stressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I was living in a-- in a poor city. Small. Wasn't much to do. Mostly people live. In the neighborhood, going with the boys, playing football, playing tennis, going skating, going to the park. We did the best we could find. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add about life--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before the war? What changed? How did you know that there was a war?

speaker: interviewee
text: How you know that's a change? Well, in 1939, I think the first week in September, the German armies attacked Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The planes came in and start throwing bombs. So the start bombing our city. First we heard on the radio. Warsaw was bombed. Krakow was bombed. Next day, planes came in to our city and throw several bombs. Damaged a few buildings. And before we turned around, on the third days, the Nazi armies marched in and occupied our city. And after several days, we heard that the whole country was occupied by the Germans. So it didn't-- so they didn't have much trouble to occupy Poland. They came in from all around Poland.
